nativeRange.habitat: Permanent lowland swamps and small, slow-moving coastal rainforest streams with soft, acidic, tannin-stained water and dense vegetation

diet.notes: Micropredator; generally does best on small live and frozen foods. Frozen daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms, and blackworms are commonly accepted and recommended for best color and conditioning. Tank-bred specimens have been reported to accept quality micro-pellets and flake food, but live or frozen foods are generally advised as the primary diet.

avoidWith: fin-nipping species (males have ornate tail extensions vulnerable to damage)
other male killifish in small tanks

imageCandidates.notes: Alexander Prokoshev, 1743x1307. Tack-sharp adult male on clean background showing every diagnostic feature: orange-gold body with red spotting, ornate lyretail with white filament extensions and dark-red fin bands.
KKPCW, 2900x2175. Brilliant orange gold-strain male in a natural planted/driftwood setup; sharp full-body view with lyretail and white filament fin tips.
lienyuan lee, 2393x1795. Sharp vibrant male with distinctive yellow-and-red banded fins and full lyretail; shows a slightly different fin colour form for variety.

summary: Colorful West African non-annual killifish with a distinctive lyretail and warm orange-to-gold body. Generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly killifish, though cooler water requirements and escape risk warrant some attention.
careNotes:
Unlike annual killifish such as Nothobranchius, the Lyretail Killifish inhabits permanent water bodies — lowland swamps and slow coastal streams — and does not undergo a seasonal diapause (the dormant egg phase that annual species use to survive dry seasons). Captive lifespans of 2–3 years are commonly reported, and some sources indicate that over 3 years is achievable in well-maintained conditions.

Temperature is one of the more important considerations with this species. The Lyretail Killifish is generally advised to be kept cooler than typical tropical fish, with a target range of around 21–24°C. Most specialist sources align on this range, and temperatures above 25°C are generally not recommended for long-term keeping.

A secure, tight-fitting lid is considered essential. This species is a capable jumper, and in smaller or shallow tanks the escape risk is particularly high.

Several ornamental color strains are commonly available in the hobby, including the Gold strain (an orange-gold body with red spotting, and the most widely kept form), the Orange strain (deeper orange tones), and the Chocolate strain (a brown-olive base with red spots). The wild type closely resembles the Gold strain, and all color forms share the same care requirements.

Color intensity has been observed to improve with peat extract in the water — which mimics the natural blackwater biotope — and dimmer, indirect lighting. Spawning success also tends to improve in soft, slightly acidic conditions; a pH of 6.0–6.5 is commonly recommended for breeding.

A pair (one male, one female) or a trio (one male, two females) is the grouping most commonly advised. Males of this species tend to be less aggressive toward each other than the related Fundulopanchax gardneri (Steel-blue Killifish): multiple males can coexist in a well-planted tank with sufficient visual breaks, though they will display and occasionally chase one another. Keeping two females per male is generally recommended to reduce male harassment of individual females.

Soft, acidic water with low carbonate hardness (kH 0–5) is generally preferred. Gentle filtration — a sponge filter or air-driven filter — is recommended to avoid excessive flow. Peat moss added to the filter or used in a breeding mop setup is beneficial for conditioning both the water and the fish.

For comparison, the related Fundulopanchax gardneri (Steel-blue Killifish) tends to be slightly hardier and tolerates a wider pH and temperature range, while males of that species tend to be more aggressive toward each other than Lyretail males. The Lyretail is generally considered the gentler and slightly more delicate of the two.
breedingNotes:
Breeding is generally considered moderate in difficulty and is regarded as accessible for aquarists new to killifish. The Lyretail Killifish is a plant and mop spawner, sometimes called an egg-hanger: eggs are individually deposited among fine-leaved plants, Java moss, or synthetic spawning mops rather than scattered in open water. During active spawning, a pair commonly produces roughly 10–20 eggs per day over a 2-week period.

Eggs can be left to incubate in water, where they typically hatch in approximately 10–20 days at 21–24°C, or they can be removed and stored semi-dry on peat moss, hatching in around 18 days when re-wetted. Fry are relatively large at hatching and have been reported to accept newly hatched brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii) immediately, though offering infusoria or commercial micro-foods for the first day or two may improve early survival. Adults will readily consume both eggs and fry, so separating eggs or raising fry in a dedicated grow-out container is generally advised. Small, frequent water changes — approximately 10–20% every 2–3 days — are commonly recommended to support fry growth.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, family/order, adult size (6.0 cm TL), native range (Angola, Gabon, Cameroon, Congo), temperature (21–24°C), pH (6.0–7.0), and hardness (5–12 dH). SeriouslyFish's 32°C max temperature was considered an outlier inconsistent with FishBase and user-provided data; the FishBase range of 21–24°C was adopted.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Top 5 Killifish
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~6 cm / 2.5 in), diet (bloodworms, brine shrimp, Vibra Bites), tank size guidance (10–20 gallon / 38–76L for pairs/trios), and general behavior notes.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Admiring Aphyosemion australe
additional.notes: Third source used for lifespan (2–3 years), temperature preference (25°C / 76–78°F cited; slightly warmer than FishBase consensus), pH guidance (6.5–7.0 preferred), and husbandry notes on diet and behavior. Note: TFH states the species is not prone to jumping, which disagrees with SeriouslyFish and general killifish guidance; the majority-source position (escape risk = high) was retained.

fish.breedingNotes: The Lyretail Killifish is an egg-hanger that deposits individual eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Eggs incubate in water for approximately 10–20 days, and fry have been reported to accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii from the start. See breedingNotes for full details.
